Parties state appearance. The Court announces the tentative ruling. The Court states a timely notice of contest the TR was received.

ARGUED. Ms. Gianini requests the court adopts its TR; analysis is correct, requests to adopt TR as to sanctions. Court comments. ARGUED. Counsel for Cross-complainant Mr. Kim Descamps contests implied contract claim, conversion, fraud. The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

1 - CROSS DEFENDANTs BAUMAN LANDSCAPE & CONSTRUCTION, INC., ANGELA BAUMAN, and MICHAEL BAUMAN DEMURRER to CROSS COMPLAINT.

Cross-defendants Bauman Landscape & Construction, Inc., Michael Bauman and Angela Bauman's demurrer to the second, fourth and fifth causes of action in the cross-complaint filed by cross-complainant Bay Area Concrete LLC is sustained with ten calendar days leave to amend as to all three causes of action.

The second cause of action for breach of the implied covenant fails to allege any breach of an implied term and is entirely duplicative of the breach of oral contract claim.

The fourth cause of action for conversion fails to allege any wrongful act apart from the alleged breach of oral contract and thus, as currently alleged, is not cognizable per the economic loss rule.

The fifth cause of action for fraud fails to allege either a claim for promissory fraud or fraud by concealment. A promissory fraud claim fails because no facts are alleged showing lack of intent to perform the alleged false promise. A claim for fraud by concealment fails because the allegedly concealed facts (the Baumans' failure to perform their contract with GSW and their contract with GSW forbid the Baumans' contract with BAC) are not material given BAC's allegation that the Baumans received all event tickets that they allegedly agreed to transfer to BAC.

DEMURRER to Amended CROSS COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Sep-20-2024 Overruled
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances via Zoom:
Zachary Flood, Esq. of Lewis & Llewellyn LLP, Tel. 415.800.0590, appearing for Plaintiff Golden State Warriors, LLC as servicer and marketing agent;
No appearance by any other party or counsel.

Hearing re: CROSS-DEFENDANT BAY AREA CONCRETE LLC DBA SLURRY SOLUTIONS AND BAY AREA CONCRETE RECYCLING'S DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED CROSS COMPLAINT.

This matter is not contested. The Court adopts its tentative ruling as follows:

Cross defendant Bay Area Concrete LLC's (BAC) demurrer to defendant Bauman Landscape & Construction, Inc.'s (Bauman) first amended cross complaint (FACC) is overruled.

Plaintiff Golden State Warriors, LLC (GSW) sued Bauman for allegedly defaulting on a contract to license a suite from 2019 to 2030. Bauman cross-complained against GSW and BAC, alleging GSW and BAC induced it to enter the license by falsely representing BAC would be a partner responsible for half the license fees. (FACC para. 10.) BAC now demurrers to the fourth through seventh causes of action (COA) for (4) promissory fraud, (5) breach of contract, (6) bad faith, and (7) equitable indemnity.

First, BAC argues the contract-based claims (COAs 5 and 6) are barred by the statute of frauds (SOF). It goes undisputed that the SOF applies under Civil Code 1624(a)(1), for contracts "not to be performed within a year." Indeed, this is an agreement to license a suite for over a decade. Bauman, however, adequately pleads compliance with the SOF. The SOF does not require a written contract; a note or memorandum subscribed by the party to be charged is adequate. (Civ. Code 1624(a); Sterling v. Taylor (2007) 40 Cal.4th 757, 765.) Here, Bauman alleges it entered into an oral agreement with GSW and BAC on April 5, 2018, that was later confirmed in writing via "numerous written electronic communications" memorializing the alleged 4/5/18 oral agreement. (FACC para. 7.) These allegations suffice at the pleading stage to show compliance with the SOF via a note or memorandum. Whether or not the specific emails contain enough information (e.g., essential terms, subscribed by the party to be charged) pose factual questions that cannot be decided at the pleadings stage. Additionally, BAC argues this is not a qualified financial contract that is exempt from the SOF under section 1624(b). This argument is unavailing. Bauman does not claim to be exempt from the SOF but rather pleads compliance with it.

Second, BAC argues that Bauman has not plead essential elements of its fraud claim (COA 4) with the requisite specificity. Among other elements, promissory fraud requires intent not to perform at the time the promise was made and reasonable reliance. (Muraoka v. Budget Rent-A-Car, Inc. (1984) 160 Cal.App.3d 107, 119.) BAC cites Tenzer v. Superscope (1985) 39 Cal.3d 18, 30, for the proposition that something more than alleging nonperformance is required to plead intent not to perform. Tenzer, however, is a summary judgment case. At the pleadings stage, "the averment that the representation was made with the intent to deceive the plaintiff, or any other general allegation with similar purport, is sufficient." (5 Witkin, California Procedure (6th ed. 2024) Pleading, section 725.) So, Bauman's allegations that "BAC had no intention of honoring these false promises" suffice. (FACC para. 38.) Similarly, Bauman's allegations of reasonable reliance are adequate at the pleadings stage. (FACC 41.) As to specificity, Bauman adequately pleads that representatives made a false promise orally, by phone, and by electronic communication in April 2018. Finally, Bauman pleads a timely claim under the discovery rule.

Accordingly, BAC's demurrer to Bauman's FACC is overruled.

Order signed.
